metric_value: Over 9.5 million employees work off-site in 1997
notes: Aberdeen cites current telecommuter population as market context

metric_value: Telecommuting projected to exceed 15 million by the year 2000
notes: Aberdeen forecasts telecommuting growth of approximately 58% by 2000

metric_value: Actual telecommuting was approximately 10% of workforce (roughly 13-14M workers) by early 2000s; projection directionally correct though definition-dependent
notes: BLS data and Gallup indicate 10% telecommuted in mid-1990s rising to 17% by early 2000s; 15M projection was broadly accurate

metric_value: Remote control is superior to remote node for large database-oriented applications over low-bandwidth connections
notes: Aberdeen clearly positions remote control as better telecommuting architecture vs Windows built-in remote node

metric_value: Remote node does not permit end-users to remotely operate desktop applications or allow MIS diagnostic control
notes: Aberdeen identifies key limitation of Windows built-in remote node for telecommuting

metric_value: SpeedSync transfers only changed file portions reducing transfer times by as much as 80%
notes: Key quantitative performance claim for LapLink's file synchronization technology

metric_value: Telecommuting increases employee retention and productivity between 10 to 30 percent
notes: Aberdeen cites productivity and retention benefit range for telecommuting programs

metric_value: Traveling Software recognized as well-established market leader in file transfer and remote control functionality
notes: Aberdeen endorses LapLink's market leadership position in remote computing utilities

metric_value: Traveling Software renamed LapLink Software ~2004; pivoted from remote control to PC migration (PCmover); remote control market lost to RDP and VPN; company survived through product pivot
notes: LapLink Software still active as of 2026; PCmover is industry standard for PC migration but original remote control use case is legacy

metric_value: Microsoft RDP (Terminal Services) and Citrix MetaFrame became dominant remote access solutions by 2001; LapLink's dial-up remote control approach became niche
notes: Enterprise market shifted to server-side remote desktop (Citrix/RDP) rather than LapLink's client-host dial-up remote control model

metric_value: LapLink provides effective economical remote access addressing major stumbling blocks of remote information access file transfer and synchronization
notes: Aberdeen's overall positive conclusion on LapLink's telecommuting value

metric_value: Separate caller passwords optional password protection three access levels keyboard/mouse disabling screen blanking callback feature and Event Log
notes: Aberdeen highlights security as key LapLink strength for IT/MIS administrators

metric_value: IT must enable remote use of bandwidth-hungry applications economically over low-bandwidth dial-in connections while addressing multiple protocols and security
notes: Aberdeen correctly frames the bandwidth vs. application richness tension as core IT challenge

metric_value: Broadband adoption post-2000 eliminated dial-up constraints; VPN became standard remote access; LapLink's bandwidth optimization features became less compelling as line speeds increased 100x
notes: Broadband DSL and cable modems changed the telecommuting connectivity landscape; dial-up constraints LapLink solved became irrelevant

metric_value: Flexibility flexibility for dependent care alternatives to relocation air quality legislation compliance cited as drivers
notes: Aberdeen catalogs non-productivity reasons organizations adopt telecommuting
